questions.question: Explain the difference between fork() and vfork() system calls.
keyPoints: fork() creates a copy of parent's address space for child
vfork() does NOT copy - child shares parent's address space
Parent is suspended during vfork() until child exits or calls exec()
vfork() is faster but dangerous if misused

questions.question: What are the return values of vfork() in parent and child processes?
keyPoints: Parent receives child's PID (positive integer)
Child receives 0
On error, returns -1 and sets errno

questions.question: Why is vfork() considered dangerous? What undefined behavior can occur?
keyPoints: Child shares parent's address space
If child modifies data without calling exec(), parent data is corrupted
If child returns from calling function, it corrupts parent's stack
Modern systems may block dangerous operations

questions.question: What is copy-on-write (COW) technique used in fork()? How does it make fork() efficient?
keyPoints: COW delays actual copying of pages until modification
Initially, parent and child share same pages (read-only)
When either modifies a page, a private copy is made
This makes fork() nearly as efficient as vfork() for many cases

questions.question: Write the typical usage pattern of vfork() in a C program.
keyPoints: pid = vfork();
if (pid == 0) { // child process
// MUST call exec() here
execl(...)
_exit(1); // if exec fails
} else if (pid > 0) { // parent
wait(NULL); // wait for child

questions.question: Why was vfork() originally introduced in Unix? Is it still recommended for use?
keyPoints: Introduced when memory was expensive and limited
Avoided overhead of copying entire address space
Optimized for fork()+exec() pattern
NOT recommended now - deprecated in POSIX.1-2008
Modern COW fork() provides similar efficiency with better safety

questions.question: What happens to the parent process during vfork()? Explain why this blocking is necessary.
keyPoints: Parent is suspended/blocked until child exits or calls exec()
Blocking is necessary because child uses parent's address space
If parent continued, both could modify memory simultaneously
This would cause data corruption and undefined behavior
